content.text: Community members raised questions about V3 development progress throughout the week, asking whether current work is focused on infrastructure stabilization or if the full autonomous agent workflow stack is close to being publicly exposed. They also asked about the integration status of the new identity and AgentID system. Shaw mentioned the current setup uses Qwen with optimizations and that a fine-tuning pipeline is being developed to improve model performance for calling actions and planning. No confirmed V3 release date was provided.

Details about the Eliza-1 model series were shared in the general discussion channel. The series is based exclusively on Qwen3.5 and includes models ranging from 0.6B, capable of running offline on any phone, up to a 27B model with a 1 million token context window. A new 0.8B model is currently being trained on H200 hardware. The series is designed to cover hardware targets from mobile phones to servers and workstations.

A developer named sailorpepe.eth reported that the v2 plugin registry infrastructure appears to be unavailable, with both the elizaos-plugins/registry repo and plugins.elizacloud.ai returning 404 errors. They had a third-party plugin ready for submission and asked whether to hold their pull request or if a staging area exists. Core developer Odilitime acknowledged the issue and suggested the team may revert to accepting pull requests directly to the elizaOS/eliza repository.

ReaWorks shared a project called the Pay or Burn escrow protocol deployed on Base Mainnet, designed for autonomous AI agents. The protocol uses a game-theoretic mechanism where if two agents cannot agree on a task outcome, the escrowed funds are burned, eliminating incentives for malicious behavior. Key architectural choices include 100 percent pull-payment to avoid issues with reverting receiver contracts, no user interface with only a raw smart contract and SDKs in Python and C++, and burn logic that destroys expected value for bad actors. ReaWorks also discussed how agent frameworks like elizaOS should interface with payment and settlement flows without embedding excessive trust logic into the agent runtime itself.

A community member working on business development for GODL, a gamified on-chain mining protocol on Solana, reached out to explore potential integration with elizaOS. GODL exposes its system through SDKs, websocket streams, automation hooks, and a public skill.md file designed for agent and tool integrations. Potential use cases include autonomous mining and staking strategies, agent-managed wallets, competing mining behaviors, and public leaderboards. Odilitime responded positively, noting that the skill.md file could be used as-is.

A discussion emerged around a reported partnership between the Secure Blockchain Development Corp and the Eliza Foundation, aimed at commercializing an Agentic SME solution using the ElizaOS framework. One user described it as a Canadian listed entity deal with a 30-day timeline for a v1 and approximately 90 days for full deployment. Another user expressed skepticism, suggesting nothing had been formally signed, which prompted a rebuttal defending the legitimacy and outlined timeline of the partnership.

The results of the auto.fun AI Sprint competition were shared, announcing winning teams after weeks of building, community voting, and live demos. First place went to Build4, second place to Clawdyland, with a third place also mentioned. Separately, a concept described as FIFA for AI Agents was shared in the coders channel, referring to a Football Fantasy league designed for AI Agents.

A user named samuel1 raised concerns about missing the migration deadline for ai16z to ElizaOS. They mentioned having sent an email but received no response, and asked for assistance from admins or community members to help with the migration process. Community members also flagged suspicious activity in the Discord, with one user suggesting an admin account may have been compromised. Odilitime clarified that the project has no admins and attributed the suspicious activity to scammers.

Development on May 17, 2026 centered on hardening cross-platform infrastructure across three main areas: Live USB stability, Android local-inference, and CI/CD consolidation. The team advanced the Live USB architecture with production-ready update manifests, systemd-based health checks, refined branded launcher labels, and validated root-mode boot configurations. For Android local-inference and voice features, the team stabilized native loader integration for stock Android APKs, enabled native TTS playback, improved voice hint forwarding for streaming plugins, and added long-generation support for Android direct paths. CI/CD improvements included resolving Docker build failures by consolidating workflows and merging image configuration files. Two issues require urgent team attention: the OpenVINO Runtime RFC needs input to finalize recommender logic and prevent accidental LLM routing, and the Speculative Decoding SWA Gate requires ongoing validation of a new fallback patch across 27B-class models. Two issues were closed: M-RoPE position validation and speculative decoding compatibility on Lunar Lake/Vulkan were resolved, and an investigation into Qwen2.5-14B bandwidth performance concluded with no persistent kernel regression found.
